text: During the robotic right hemicolectomy, the surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply dissect and remove scar tissue along the lateral abdominal wall, which is a result of a prior appendectomy. The cecal polyp is visibly tattooed.

The surgeon begins medial to lateral colon mobilization by opening the peritoneum below the ileocolic pedicle, creating a window for vessel division. Using a robotic vessel sealer, the ileocolic pedicle is divided. The surgeon continues in the avascular plane, sweeping the duodenum and pancreas inferiorly, aiming for a mostly bloodless operation up to the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon completes the hepatic flexure takedown and divides vessels along the lateral abdominal wall, maintaining an avascular plane for a mostly bloodless procedure. The colon mesentery is divided up to the transverse colon, with the omentum being dissected from the anti-mesenteric surface to prepare for future anastomosis.

The surgeon administers ICG to assess colon perfusion before placing a stapler across the colon for division. The mesentery of the ileum is divided at the proximal transection point, ensuring blood flow is maintained while harvesting lymph nodes. Lateral attachments are also being taken down.

The surgeon uses a blunt instrument, the robotic vessel sealer, to sweep and ensure no retroperitoneal structures like the ureter or kidney have entered the dissection field. The surgeon continues to take down film attachments along the lateral abdominal wall and the white line of Toldt.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to divide the ileum after confirming adequate perfusion with a second dose of ICG, which causes the tissue to light up green. The specimen is then passed above the liver for safekeeping. The transverse colon is positioned, and the omentum is taken down to clear space for the colotomy and the upcoming iliocolonic anastomosis.

The surgeon brings the ileum into opposition with the transverse colon, placing a stay stitch at the apex to manage tension during the anastomosis. Colotomies are made in both the ileum and colon, using a mix of cutting and coagulation techniques. The stapler is positioned for the anastomosis, and despite minor bleeding controlled with a bipolar device, the limbs are maneuvered into alignment before firing the stapler.

The surgeon uses a V-lock barbed suture to close the common channel of the anastomosis, starting at the bottom corner to ensure a secure closure and prevent leaks. The suture is placed mucosa to mucosa, progressing upwards to the superior apex, and then a lumbar suture is applied back down from top to bottom, completing a two-layer closure.

The surgeon focuses on ensuring the tissue is in opposition while covering the common channel with limber sutures. The anastomosis appears widely patent, and the blood supply is adequate. The surgeon checks the anastomosis, noting a small amount of omentum positioned to cover and buttress the area.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and retrieves the specimen through a twelve millimeter port, marking the conclusion of the surgical procedure.
